value:
v1.14.0 development plan. PHASED, dataset-driven, theme-unresolved. Current state as of 2026-04-23: Phase 1 (soak) just started; v1.13.0 shipped today.

DISCIPLINE: the dogfooding-real-usage addendum argues the loop tells you not just what to build but the ORDER. This plan honors that — we don't pick the theme until the next dataset says which direction has evidence.

PHASE 1 — SOAK (2026-04-23 → ~2026-05-14, ~3 weeks)
Let v1.13.0 accumulate real-usage data before committing to a theme. Target dataset size: ≥500 MCP calls across ≥50 sessions (matches the v1.12→v1.13 cycle's data volume).

Acceptance signals to watch (v1.13.0's promises):
- Empty-codex_get rate drops from 48% baseline (#92 core acceptance).
- aliasResolved reliably captured on codex_run + codex_copy (#93/#94).
- context.next_session picks up organic use (#91 adoption signal — instructions nudge agents to write it).
- Seed-quality lint holds 0 false-positives on the tuned-against-real-codex baseline (#82).
- Topology pair counts emerge past zero (#83 — needs real focused-read history to show anything).

At end of soak: mine audit.jsonl + telemetry.jsonl, file new issues for findings, then run Phase 2.

PHASE 2 — THEME PICK (~2026-05-14)
v1.14.0 milestone currently has 13 candidates (5 moved there 2026-04-23 from the original v1.13.0 trim, 7 pre-existing from the 2026-04-10 milestone creation, plus #96 the #95 CI follow-up). Four candidate themes:

  A. Metadata maturity — #45 attribution, #84 entry health, #42 git-aware freshness. Deeper work; aligns with agent-first north star (who/when matters for multi-agent workflows). #84 needs design-first like #91 did.

  B. Search / discoverability — #13 fuzzy finder, #43 boolean search. Lower architectural impact; improves interactive UX.

  C. Distribution — #6 Fish/PowerShell completion, #49 Windows testing, #50 npx zero-install, #51 IDE extensions. Friction-reduction for first-time users; marketing-heavy; lower complexity per item.

  D. Polish / performance — #44 richer data types, #47 ccli diff, #48 performance at scale. Defensive / maintenance-y.

Dataset should bias the pick:
- Metadata conversations in usage patterns → A.
- Agents struggling to locate entries (miss-path signal, low hit rates in codex_find) → B.
- New-user onboarding questions on Github issues / discussions → C.
- Response-time complaints, freeze-like behavior → D.

Pick the theme anchor + ≤5 issues total. Trim rest to v1.15 backlog (unmilestone). Don't commit to more than one release-cycle can deliver.

PHASE 3 — DESIGN + BUILD (~2026-05-15 onward)
Design-first for the anchor issue if it's not already concrete (e.g. #84 needs shape; #45 is probably spec-ready). Build. Follow the #91 playbook: design conversation → scoped plan → one feature per commit → tests → live verify → ship.

PHASE 4 — CI / INDIE ITEMS (any time — not on theme critical path)
#96 is the only one on a hard deadline: v3 action re-evaluation before 2026-06-02 Node-24 forced cutover. If theme work drags past May, #96 still ships on its own.

RELEASE TARGET
No calendar date yet. Cycle length was ~3 weeks from v1.12.2 (2026-04-16) → v1.13.0 (2026-04-23), but v1.13.0 was concentrated. v1.14.0 with real feature work + soak phase is more like 4-6 weeks from today. Don't over-commit — SHIP shape of v1.14.0 will be known after Phase 2.

DOGFOODING PART THREE
Expected alongside v1.14.0 tag. Narrative TBD: "the loop closes again" if acceptance signals landed, "adjusting the loop" if the data shows the agent-first bets didn't play out. Either is a legitimate post.

DELIBERATE DEFERRALS
Not in v1.14.0 unless Phase 2 pulls them in:
- All non-theme candidates move to v1.15 backlog.
- Go rewrite (referenced in ROADMAP.md as long-term) — not scoped here.
- Any new issues surfaced during soak — filed but triaged per theme.

ANTI-PATTERN WARNINGS
- Don't pre-commit to a theme before Phase 1 data. The addendum's lesson: the loop tells you the order. Skipping Phase 1 throws that away.
- Don't let the candidate list sprawl past 5 shipped items. v1.13.0's 11-item closure was possible because the issues were concentrated and dataset-primed; that's an outlier, not a template.
- Don't skip the design pass on #84 if it's picked. Handoff (#91) showed that 30 minutes of design conversation avoided a week of rework.
